title: The flow of local quantum fluids: Conservation laws and vertex corrections from many-body linear-response theory with local self-energy
abstract: In non-diffusive conduction regimes of strongly correlated quantum electron systems, electromagnetic perturbations simultaneously probe the electronic dynamics in time and space: the exchanged energy $\hbar ω$ excites retarded, i.e., frequency-dependent, many-body interactions, while the probing spatial modulation renders the response spatially nonlocal, i.e., dependent on the external wave vector $\vec{q}$. This work determines the exact nonlocal electrodynamic response of such dynamical quantum fluids under the assumptions of local, frequency-dependent interactions and charge/mass conservation. The latter is ensured by Bethe-Salpeter equations for renormalized interaction vertices, entering the Kubo formalism for two-particle correlation functions (e.g., for density, currents, momentum, stress). Within such framework, it is shown that vertex corrections generally vanish at $q=0$ for single-particle dispersions with inversion symmetry and for bare interaction vertices that are odd with respect to specific point group transformations in momentum space, including inversion for vector vertices, and mirror reflections or two- or higher-fold rotations for tensor vertices. In addition, for quadratic dispersion vertex corrections identically vanish from the current-current correlation function, at any momentum $\vec{q}$ and frequency $ω$. The robustness of these criteria against further symmetry breaking, multiband effects, and additionally imposing momentum conservation, is discussed, with application to the Hall viscosity of Landau levels. Explicit expressions for generic nonlocal correlation functions are derived for Fermi liquids (with well-defined quasiparticle peaks) and non-Fermi liquids (devoid of quasiparticles), for arbitrary local self-energies.
summary: This paper develops a theoretical framework to calculate the nonlocal electrodynamic response of quantum fluids with local self-energies. It identifies specific symmetry criteria under which many-body vertex corrections vanish, providing exact results for transport coefficients like conductivity and viscosity. This is crucial for accurately modeling non-diffusive, hydrodynamic transport in strongly correlated materials.
main_problem: Determining the exact nonlocal electrodynamic response of strongly correlated quantum electron systems and establishing symmetry-based criteria for when vertex corrections vanish in the local self-energy limit.
main_result: The paper proves that vertex corrections vanish at q=0 if the bare vertex is odd under a symmetry operation that preserves the dispersion, and specifically that for quadratic isotropic dispersion, corrections to the current-current correlation function vanish at any q and frequency.
method: Many-body linear-response theory using the Kubo formalism, Bethe-Salpeter equations for renormalized vertices, and the Luttinger-Ward functional within a DMFT-like local self-energy framework.
model_or_system: Strongly correlated quantum electron systems, including Fermi liquids, non-Fermi liquids, strange metals, and Mott insulators, analyzed in the limit of local, frequency-dependent interactions.
key_observables: Two-particle correlation functions including density, currents (electric, momentum, thermal), momentum, stress, Hall viscosity, and kinetic stress tensor.
important_parameters: Momentum transfer (q), exchanged frequency (omega), electronic dispersion (epsilon_k), and point group symmetries (inversion, mirror, rotation).
main_assumptions: Local, frequency-dependent (retarded) self-energy (DMFT limit), translational invariance in space and time, and adherence to charge and mass conservation laws.
figures_summary: Figure 1 illustrates a 2D electron liquid responding to an oscillating electric field; Figure 2 shows the Bethe-Salpeter equation structure; Figure 3 compares 3D crystal symmetries (e.g., cubic SrTiO3) and their effect on shear-stress vertex corrections.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of nonlocal response, establishes the many-body framework using the Bethe-Salpeter equation, derives symmetry-based selection rules for vanishing vertex corrections, applies these rules to various tensors and crystal symmetries, and provides explicit expressions for Fermi and non-Fermi liquids.
why_interesting: It provides exact symmetry-based selection rules for many-body vertex corrections, which is fundamental for anyone studying the transport properties and hydrodynamic regimes of strongly interacting quantum fluids.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. Artistic illustration of the nonlocal response of a 2D elec- tron liquid (blue waves), embedded in a 2D lattice (red and green spheres), to an oscillating electric field E(r, t) applied at coordi- nate r (green arrow). Electrons are assumed to interact through a retarded local self-energy ΣR(ω) stemming from microscopic many-body interactions (inset in orange dashed box).

FIG. 2. Graphical representation of the Bethe-Salpeter equation (4) for the renormalized vertex ΓˆΘ,α(k,q, iωm, iΩn) associated with the observable ˆΘ, assuming a local irreducible two-particle vertex Λ(iωm, iω′ m, iΩn).

FIG. 3. Schematic illustration of the relation between symmetry of a 3D crystal structure and vanishing criteria for shear-stresses vertex corrections in the DMFT limit, according to the Bethe-Salpeter equation (4) and Eq. (21). (a) Unit cell of cubic SrTiO3, showing mirror symmetry planes (light-blue shaded planes), and therefore yielding vanishing vertex corrections for shear stresses. (b) Unit cell of triclinic Mg2B2O5, possessing no mirror symmetry planes perpendicular to any principal axis; a hypothetical one-band metal of the same symmetry, or a symmetry-degraded metallic crystal subject to mechanical deformation – see also Sec. VIII D – acquires vertex corrections for shear...

FIG. 4. An example of noncentrosymmetric crystal where breaking of parity and time-reversal symmetry invalidates the k-inversion criteria (12) and (15) for vanishing vertex corrections at q = 0. (a) Unit cell of GaAs showing the absence of a lattice inversion symmetry. (b) Bandstructure of the 2-band toy-model Hamiltonian (F1) for GaAs in the absence of spin-orbit coupling (γD = 0) and of Zeeman splitting (B = 0). (c) Bandstructure for the GaAs Hamiltonian (F1) with Dresselhaus spin-orbit coupling and Zeeman splitting, which breaks both parity and time-reversal symmetry and shows different dispersions εk,σ ̸= ε−k,σ′ along the directions X ↔L in reciprocal space.
